31.title: Baby Processes Information
31.subtitle: All five senses are working.
31.keyDevDesc: Baby can hear, see light, feel, taste, and smell.
babyDevBullets: All five senses are fully functional.
Baby is processing information from the environment.
Weight gain accelerates—baby will gain about a half-pound a week until birth.
emotionalScripts: Whatever happens on labor day, I will be right by your side.
practicalTasks: Start packing the hospital bag (clothes, toiletries, snacks).
Assemble the stroller and practice folding/unfolding it.
relationshipIdeas: Discuss your visitor policy for the hospital and first few weeks home.

bondingTasks.title: Pack the Bag
bondingTasks.text: Pack your dad go-bag: long phone charger, snacks, change of clothes.

mustBuy: Extra-long phone charging cable for the hospital
medicalRoadmap: Discuss Group B Strep (GBS) testing, which usually happens around week 36.
takeaways: Baby is getting heavy.
The hospital bag should be packed and waiting by the door.

32.title: Practicing Breathing
32.subtitle: Lungs are doing practice reps.
32.keyDevDesc: Baby expands lungs, inhaling amniotic fluid.
32.dadPriorityDesc: Plan for meals and help after birth.
32.partnerSymptomsDesc: Baby's head may be pressing down.
babyDevBullets: Baby is doing 'practice breathing' by inhaling and exhaling amniotic fluid.
Lanugo (the fine body hair) is starting to fall off.
Skin is becoming fully opaque and smooth.

symptomTable.name: Pelvic Pressure
symptomTable.why: Baby getting heavier and moving lower

emotionalScripts: I've got the meal train set up so we don't have to cook those first few weeks.
practicalTasks: Organize a 'meal train' with friends/family or freeze meals for the postpartum period.
Stock up on household essentials (toilet paper, paper plates, pet food).
relationshipIdeas: Spend a weekend afternoon batch-cooking and freezing meals.

bondingTasks.title: Freezer Prep
bondingTasks.text: Cook a massive batch of chili or lasagna to freeze.

medicalRoadmap: Some doctors do a late third-trimester ultrasound to check baby's size and position.
takeaways: Lungs are doing vital practice.
Postpartum survival relies on having food ready.

33.title: Immune System Boost
33.subtitle: Getting antibodies from mom.
33.keyDevDesc: Mom's antibodies cross the placenta to protect baby.
babyDevBullets: Baby is receiving a massive transfer of antibodies from mom to fight off infections after birth.
Pupils can constrict and dilate in response to light.
Amniotic fluid is at its maximum level.

symptomTable.name: Clumsiness
symptomTable.why: Relaxin hormone loosening joints + large belly
symptomTable.help: Clear tripping hazards around the house

emotionalScripts: I've cleared the hallways so you don't have to navigate around boxes.
practicalTasks: Anchor heavy furniture (dressers, bookshelves, TVs) to the walls.
Set up the baby monitor and test the range.
relationshipIdeas: Watch a movie and simply hold hands.

bondingTasks.title: Monitor Test
bondingTasks.text: Test the baby monitor from the bedroom to the nursery.

medicalRoadmap: Review the signs of preterm labor: regular contractions, water breaking, bleeding.
takeaways: Baby's immune system is getting primed.
Anchor your furniture now—toddlers climb everything.

34.title: Fingernails Fully Formed
34.subtitle: Baby is getting ready for their debut.
34.keyDevDesc: Central nervous system is maturing rapidly.
34.partnerSymptomsDesc: Feeling as tired as the first trimester.
babyDevBullets: Fingernails have reached the tips of the fingers (they may even need a trim at birth).
Boys' testicles are descending into the scrotum.
Vernix coating gets thicker.

symptomTable.name: Extreme Fatigue
symptomTable.why: Carrying significant extra weight and poor sleep
symptomTable.help: Encourage her to start maternity leave early if possible

emotionalScripts: I bought all your postpartum supplies so you don't have to think about it.
practicalTasks: Assemble a postpartum recovery basket for her bathroom (peri bottle, dermoplast, giant pads).
Install the baby's bassinet next to your bed.
relationshipIdeas: Give her a gentle foot and calf massage every single night.

bondingTasks.title: Bassinet Setup
bondingTasks.text: Set up the safe sleep space.

mustBuy: Maxi pads, Dermoplast pain spray, Perineal bottle
optionalBuy: Adult diapers (many moms prefer these to giant pads)
medicalRoadmap: Prepare for the Group B Strep (GBS) swab test next week or week 36.
takeaways: She is exhausted; treat her like she's running a marathon.
The postpartum bathroom kit is an essential dad task.

35.title: Dropping Into Position
35.subtitle: Baby is moving into the pelvis.
35.keyDevDesc: Baby drops head-down into the pelvis.
35.dadPriorityDesc: Review when to go to the hospital.
35.partnerSymptomsDesc: Baby's head is directly on the bladder.
babyDevBullets: Baby often 'drops' (lightening) into the pelvis to prepare for birth.
Kidneys are fully developed and liver can process waste.
Fat continues to accumulate, especially around the shoulders.

symptomTable.name: Constant Urination
symptomTable.help: Map out bathrooms everywhere you go

emotionalScripts: We know the plan. When it's time, I will get us there safely.
practicalTasks: Review the 5-1-1 rule: Contractions 5 minutes apart, lasting 1 minute, for 1 hour = time to go.
Wash the baby's newborn clothes and blankets in fragrance-free detergent.
relationshipIdeas: Keep things light and calm. Anxiety is probably high.

bondingTasks.title: Baby Laundry
bondingTasks.text: Wash and fold the tiny clothes.

medicalRoadmap: GBS Swab Test: tests for bacteria that is harmless to mom but dangerous to baby during delivery.
questions: If GBS positive, what is the protocol? (Usually IV antibiotics during labor)
takeaways: Baby dropping makes breathing easier but urination constant.
Know exactly when it's time to head to the hospital.

36.title: Early Term
36.subtitle: Almost at the finish line.
36.keyDevDesc: Baby sheds the waxy coating and downy hair.
36.dadPriorityDesc: Gas in the car, bags by the door.
36.partnerSymptomsDesc: A sudden burst of energy to clean and organize.
babyDevBullets: Baby sheds the vernix and lanugo, which they swallow (this becomes the meconium poop).
Digestive system is ready for breast milk or formula.
Baby is considered 'early term' next week.

symptomTable.name: Nesting Instinct
symptomTable.why: Evolutionary drive to prepare the environment
symptomTable.help: Help her clean, but don't let her climb ladders or lift heavy things

emotionalScripts: The house is ready. We are ready.
practicalTasks: Never let the car's gas tank fall below half full.
Confirm who will take care of your pets when you go to the hospital.
relationshipIdeas: Go on your absolute final date night (if she's up for it).

bondingTasks.title: Gas Check
bondingTasks.text: Keep the tank full at all times.

medicalRoadmap: Appointments are now once a week until birth.
questions: Will the doctor perform a cervical check today?
takeaways: The baby is practically fully baked.
Logistics (gas, pets, bags) must be 100% locked in.

37.title: Early Term Reached
37.subtitle: Baby could safely arrive any day now.
37.keyDevDesc: Lungs are capable of breathing outside air.
37.dadPriorityDesc: Keep your phone loud and stay close.
babyDevBullets: Baby is officially 'early term'.
Coordination is perfected so baby can grasp things (like the umbilical cord).
Immune system is getting final upgrades.

symptomTable.name: Losing Mucus Plug
symptomTable.help: Don't panic; labor could still be days or weeks away

emotionalScripts: My phone is off silent and I will answer on the first ring.
practicalTasks: Stop traveling. Stay within an hour of the hospital.
Review the route to the hospital and alternate routes in case of traffic.
relationshipIdeas: Enjoy quiet evenings at home. Rest is the priority.

bondingTasks.title: Ringer On
bondingTasks.text: Take your phone off Do Not Disturb.

optionalBuy: Waterproof mattress protector (in case water breaks in bed)
medicalRoadmap: Weekly cervical checks may begin to see if she is dilating or effacing.
questions: Are there any signs of dilation?
takeaways: Baby is safe to be born now.
Losing the mucus plug means the body is preparing, but it's not a reason to rush to the ER.

38.title: Organs Ready
38.subtitle: Just putting on the finishing touches.
38.keyDevDesc: Final maturing of brain and lung function.
38.dadPriorityDesc: Be the anchor. Keep anxiety low.
38.partnerSymptomsDesc: So utterly done with being pregnant.
babyDevBullets: Baby's organ systems are fully functioning.
Baby is continuing to pack on fat to stay warm after birth.
Eye color is established (though it can change over the first year).

symptomTable.name: Extreme Discomfort
symptomTable.why: A full-sized baby inside a human body
symptomTable.help: Be endlessly patient. Validate her complaints.

emotionalScripts: You have done such an incredible job. Not much longer now.
practicalTasks: Ensure the house is clean.
Double-check the hospital bags and put them in the car.

bondingTasks.title: Bags in Car
bondingTasks.text: Move the go-bags to the trunk of the car.

medicalRoadmap: Weekly check. The doctor may discuss membrane sweeping if appropriate.
questions: What is our induction plan if we go past the due date?
takeaways: It is a waiting game now.
Keep her as comfortable and stress-free as possible.

39.title: Full Term
39.subtitle: Any minute now.
39.keyDevDesc: New skin forms as outer layers shed.
39.partnerSymptomsDesc: Waiting for the real contractions to start.
babyDevBullets: Officially full term!
Baby's head is likely engaged deep in the pelvis.
Endocrine system prepares to release stress hormones for birth.

symptomTable.name: False Labor
symptomTable.help: Time the contractions. If they stop when she walks or drinks water, it's false labor.

emotionalScripts: Every contraction brings us closer to meeting our baby.
practicalTasks: Download a contraction timer app on your phone.
Review the signs of labor: water breaking, bloody show, consistent 5-1-1 contractions.
relationshipIdeas: Try natural ways to encourage labor if she is up for it (walking, spicy food, intimacy).

bondingTasks.title: Timer App
bondingTasks.text: Test out the contraction timer app so you know how to use it.

medicalRoadmap: Weekly checkup. Monitoring for any signs of preeclampsia or distress.
questions: When exactly do you want us to call the on-call doctor?
takeaways: This is it.
You are the timekeeper and the calm presence.

40.title: The Due Date
40.subtitle: Eviction Notice.
40.keyDevDesc: Fully developed and ready to breathe air.
40.dadPriorityDesc: Keep her mind off the waiting.
40.partnerSymptomsDesc: Only 5% of babies are born on their due date.
babyDevBullets: Skull bones are not fused so they can overlap during the journey through the birth canal.
15% of the baby's body weight is fat.
Baby is ready for the world.
emotionalScripts: The due date is just an estimate. The baby will come when they are ready.
practicalTasks: Field texts from family asking 'Is the baby here yet?' so she doesn't have to.
Keep walking and bouncing on an exercise ball.
relationshipIdeas: Go to a movie theater or out to dinner—distract yourselves.

bondingTasks.title: Bouncer
bondingTasks.text: Act as the communication shield between her and anxious relatives.

medicalRoadmap: The OB will likely schedule an induction date for week 41 if nothing happens.
questions: What is the plan for monitoring baby if we go past the due date?
takeaways: Do not stress if the baby isn't born today.
Protect her from annoying texts.

birth.title: Labor & Delivery
birth.subtitle: The Day Everything Changes.
birth.keyDevDesc: Fluid is cleared from lungs, baby breathes air.
babyDevBullets: The journey through the birth canal squeezes fluid from the lungs.
The umbilical cord is cut, and baby takes their first breath.
Apgar scores are assessed at 1 and 5 minutes.

symptomTable.name: Active Labor
symptomTable.help: Provide counter-pressure, offer ice chips, advocate for her

emotionalScripts: Look at me. Breathe with me. You are so strong.
practicalTasks: Advocate for her birth plan preferences with the nurses.
Keep her hydrated (ice chips).
Cut the cord if you choose to do so.
relationshipIdeas: Skin-to-skin contact with your new baby as soon as possible.

bondingTasks.title: Skin to Skin
bondingTasks.text: Take your shirt off and hold your baby against your bare chest.

mustBuy: Coffee for you (it's going to be a long day)
medicalRoadmap: Labor has 3 stages: Dilation, Pushing/Birth, and Delivery of the Placenta.
takeaways: Be her rock.
You are a father now.
